problem_summary: 2016 MCM/ICM Problem D (Information networks / "Who Moved My Opinion?"). The task: model how information flows through society's communication networks, define what makes information "news," validate the model on real data, predict the relationships/capacities of society's networks in 2050, and model how public opinion changes as information spreads. This team built a multi-layer network + agent-based forwarding simulation, an economics-flavored "inherent value of information" model, a life-cycle-based network-evolution forecast, and a threshold-based opinion-change model.
models_used: Multi-layer communication network topology: each 'single-layer' network = one communication technique (newspaper, telegraph, TV, Internet, mobile web), built by superimposing Mass Communication (star topology around media nodes) and Interpersonal Communication (random-like person-to-person graph); five techniques across six historical periods stacked into a multi-layer graph

Node/edge formalism: nodes carry {type (media/person), degree, state, reception}; three node states (unknown / known / tired) governing forwarding; directed edges carry {startnode, endnode, time} where 'time' is transmission latency of that medium

Agent-based dynamic information-flow simulation with distinct forwarding rules: media nodes forward only if IV > IV_news (news threshold) and not yet tired; person nodes forward stochastically with probability p = α × IV

Inherent Value of information model via microeconomic Indifference Curves: IV = att1^x · att2^y · att3^z (Cobb-Douglas form) over three attributes — extremeness, entertainment, newness — each in [0,1], with exponents x,y,z drawn from a probability distribution to capture individual preference
Pareto-principle news filter: solving the triple integral over the attribute cube so the top 20% of value-volume defines news, yielding the threshold IV_news = 0.2154
Network-evolution forecast: exponential 'emergence of new networks' function y = ω·e^(λ(t−1870)) fit by least squares to historical invention dates, predicting ~7 networks by 2050

Life-Cycle Theory capacity model per network: piecewise — linear growth before peak (ca = ςt + ξ, anchored to emergence time and peak time), inverse decay after peak (ca = k_i / t with k_i = ca*_i · t*_i), peak capacity assumed ~100%

Opinion-change model: adds binary opinion attribute {agreement/disagreement} to nodes; Threshold Effect (threshold 0.18 from literature) flips a node's opinion once cumulative received agreement exceeds threshold; forwarding probability modified to p* = p × opinion (0 if disagree, so dissenters don't propagate)
key_techniques: Cross-disciplinary borrowing: imported Indifference Curves / Cobb-Douglas utility from microeconomics, the Pareto principle, and Product Life-Cycle Theory rather than inventing ad-hoc formulas — gives each modeling choice an external justification
Three-state node automaton (unknown/known/tired) as a tractable substitute for full epidemic SIR bookkeeping while still capturing 'forwarding fatigue'
Splitting forwarding logic by node type — deterministic news-threshold gate for media, probabilistic p=α·IV for persons — cleanly encodes the asymmetry between broadcasters and individuals

Quantitative validation against a real case: BBC Tech's 'AlphaGo beats Go champion' Facebook story, scaling the real network down 1/10000 (1 media node deg 20, 77 person nodes) and matching received-node count (real 44 vs simulated mean 43.85, SE 4.63 over 20 runs)
Least-squares fit of an exponential to historical technology-invention dates to extrapolate the number of future networks
Monte-Carlo style repetition (20 runs) to report mean and standard error rather than a single deterministic outcome
Sensitivity analysis across four levers (initial opinion, inherent value, degree-distribution standard deviation, source = media vs person) each yielding a quotable real-world maxim
why_it_won: "It is a complete, end-to-end story that answers every sub-question with a coherent single framework, then validates it against real data — the rarest thing in this problem. The team turned a vague sociology prompt into a concrete simulatable system (typed nodes, states, edge latencies, forwarding rules) and then actually ran it on a real BBC/Facebook news event, getting a near-exact match (44 vs 43.85). That empirical anchor, plus reporting standard error over 20 runs, signals scientific honesty judges reward. The cross-disciplinary scaffolding (Indifference Curves, Pareto, Life-Cycle) makes every modeling decision feel principled rather than arbitrary, and the sensitivity analysis converts dry parameter sweeps into memorable, citable insights ('go find a media,' 'stubborn minorities,' 'more homogeneous, more efficient'). The writing is confident, well-sectioned, and uses vivid framing ('A Slice of Toast' for layers, North Korea as a real-world illustration of media dominance) that makes a reader remember the paper."
section_highlights: "Summary sheet is strong: it states the validation number (44 vs 43.85, SE 4.63) and the three headline opinion findings up front, so a judge knows within 30 seconds that the model was tested and produced takeaways. Section structure marches cleanly through topology → information value → flow dynamics → validation → evolution forecast → opinion model → sensitivity → strengths/weaknesses, mirroring the problem's sub-questions in order. Memorable framing devices ('A Slice of Toast'/'Slices of Toast' for single- vs multi-layer, section titles that double as conclusions like 'Want a bigger influence, go to find a media' and 'More Homogeneous, More Efficient'). Each sensitivity result is paired with an 'Interesting finding' callout plus a 'practical significance' real-world translation. Assumptions are explicitly justified one-by-one, and the Strengths/Weaknesses section is candid (admits 8 assumptions and small validation data)."
pitfalls_or_limitations: Validation is thin and arguably circular: a single news item, a 1/10000 down-scaled network of only 77 person nodes, with key parameters (degree distributions, edge time, the three attribute values all set to 0.8) hand-tuned 'after testing several times' to fit — so the 44 vs 43.85 match partly reflects calibration, not blind prediction
Attribute values are unmeasured/arbitrary: extremeness, entertainment, newness are asserted (e.g., all 0.8) with no procedure to extract them from real content

The Pareto news-threshold derivation is mathematically loose — the stated triple integral (∫∫∫ att1·att2·att3) isn't the correct integral of the IV density over the super-level set, so IV_news = 0.2154 is at best heuristic; exponents x,y,z are also collapsed to 1 in practice

The multi-layer machinery is built up elaborately but then discarded for validation ('we simplify by using a single-layer network'), so the headline structural contribution is never actually exercised against data

2050 forecast rests on a fragile exponential fit to a handful of invention dates plus a peak-capacity = 100% assumption and an inverse-decay tail with no validation; the capacity table even lists Newspaper at 0.2778 in 2050 with little justification
Self-admitted weakness: 8 assumptions make the model abstract; several charts (e.g., the att1 and source sensitivity plots) appear idealized/synthetic rather than raw simulation output

Minor sloppiness: typos ('purples,' 'goof tool'), an OCR-garbled 3D-surface data table, mislabeled figure cross-references ('figure 9' for figure 15), and the opinion flowchart's threshold compares 'reception degree > 0.18' somewhat ambiguously
reusable_patterns: Typed-node + state-machine agent model: give nodes a type, a small finite state set (unknown/known/tired) and type-specific forwarding rules — a reusable recipe for any diffusion/contagion/adoption problem that needs more nuance than SIR but less than full ABM

Anchor every soft modeling choice to a named external theory (utility/Indifference Curve for value, Pareto for thresholds, Life-Cycle for adoption curves) — borrowing legitimacy is a repeatable way to make subjective formulas defensible to judges

Cobb-Douglas product form f = ∏ x_i^{w_i} as a go-to multi-attribute scoring function: monotone, convex indifference sets, weights = preferences — reuse for any 'combine several [0,1] attributes into one value' need

Down-scale a real large network by a fixed factor, calibrate on accessible public data (here Facebook follower/view counts), and report mean ± standard error over many runs — a lightweight validation template when full-scale data is unavailable
Turn each sensitivity-analysis result into a one-line, real-world-flavored heading/maxim plus a 'practical significance' paragraph — makes parameter sweeps memorable and quotable
Piecewise life-cycle curve (linear rise to a peak anchored at known event times, then 1/t decay) as a cheap, parameter-light way to forecast adoption/decline of competing technologies
Use vivid sectioning metaphors and conclusion-as-title headings to give a long technical paper narrative momentum and recall
